title: Micromeles coronata
taxonomy: Micromeles coronata is classified within the family Rosaceae, which includes a wide range of flowering plants commonly known as the rose family. The genus Micromeles is part of the subfamily Amygdaloideae. This subfamily is a group of closely related plants, often characterized by their fruit and flower morphology. Within the Rosaceae family, Micromeles coronata is specifically a species of flowering plant native to China. Its classification also falls under the kingdom Plantae, further categorizing it as a plant species.
uses: Micromeles coronata, also known as the Chinese hawthorn, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's leaves, flowers, and fruits are often used to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and fever. Its medicinal properties are attributed to the presence of flavonoids, alkaloids, and other bioactive compounds. In traditional Chinese medicine, Micromeles coronata is used to treat conditions such as diarrhea, dysentery, and respiratory problems. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Micromeles coronata in treating these conditions.

parts: Micromeles coronata is native to the Himalayan region and has been traditionally used for various medicinal purposes. The plant's bark, leaves, and roots are used in traditional medicine. preparations: Micromeles coronata preparations are typically used for their potential medicinal properties. intro: Micromeles coronata, also known as Chinese rowan or Chinese rowanberry, is a species of shrub in the family Rosaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Micromeles coronata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional Chinese medicine, the plant is often used to make tea, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being incorporated into herbal soups and decoctions.
